# Managing fisheries sustainably depends on international cooperation

![A front facing photo of Ernesto Jardim.](/ResourcePackages/MSC/assets/img/defaultSquareImg.jpg "Ernesto Jardim")

Ernesto Jardim

MSC Fisheries Standards Director

**Writing in our 2020 Annual Report, Ernesto Jardim talks about how collaboration across borders is essential for sustainable fishing, especially when faced with a changing climate.**

**![Large fishing boat on rough water surrounded by sea birds ](/images/default-source/msc-english/news-images/large-fishing-boat-on-rough-water-in-north-sea.jpg?sfvrsn=c9e5b795_6 "Large fishing boat on rough water in North Sea ")***Large fishing boat on rough water in North Sea istock.com/piola666*

![Large fishing boat on rough water surrounded by sea birds ](/images/default-source/msc-english/news-images/large-fishing-boat-on-rough-water-in-north-sea.jpg?sfvrsn=c9e5b795_6 "Large fishing boat on rough water in North Sea ")

Fish do not acknowledge borders – so international cooperation in fisheries management is crucial. Highly migratory species such as tuna, cross vast expanses of ocean, while others such as herring spend their lives in smaller areas of sea.

[The joint demersal fisheries certification in the North Sea](https://fisheries.msc.org/en/fisheries/joint-demersal-fisheries-in-the-north-sea-and-adjacent-waters/) illustrates how fishing organisations in different countries can come together to manage stocks that share the same ecosystem. In this case, as a condition of MSC certification, the fisheries need to put in place a monitoring system to prove they meet the landing obligation (discard ban), as in this case, it is a condition of MSC certification. Governments need to do this anyway to comply with EU law introduced in reforms of the Common Fisheries Policy. Now, regulators, fishers and markets should all be pulling in the same direction.

The need for international cooperation is going to increase as a result of climate change. Stocks of pelagic species in the North East Atlantic, such as mackerel and herring, have been moving further north because of climate change, and have been increasingly found in the waters of Greenland and Iceland. But quotas set have not reflected this change, so stocks are being overfished. As a result, all the MSC certified mackerel fisheries in the North East Atlantic had their certification suspended last year.

This is a stark illustration of how we need to manage whole ecosystems in an adaptive, scientific way, rather than managing national fish stocks independently. While individual fisheries often make great efforts to improve their sustainability, ultimately, they cannot do it alone. International management systems need to deliver too.

Celebrating and supporting sustainable fisheries

--- SOURCE 3: Why Is International Cooperation Needed for Fisheries? → Question ---
URL: https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/question/why-is-international-cooperation-needed-for-fisheries/

SUMMARY:
Cooperation ensures sustainable fisheries, addressing migratory stocks, climate change, and illegal fishing for long-term ocean health.

FULL CONTENT:
![Climate → Sustainability Directory](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Climate-Sustainability-Directory-Logotype.svg)
![Climate → Sustainability Directory](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Climate-Sustainability-Directory-Logotype.svg)
![Why Is International Cooperation Needed for Fisheries?](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/why-is-international-cooperation-needed-for-fisheries-question-image.webp)

# Why Is International Cooperation Needed for Fisheries?

![A large rock sits beneath shallow water marked with pollution reflecting ecological risk and environmental degradation emphasizing a story of environmental pollution. Detritus scatters the rock, signifying ecosystem damage through unsustainable practice which contributes to the carbon footprint effects. Water pollution, a product of climate change, dramatically affects our environment.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/polluted-waters-stones-and-reflections-visualizing-water-pollution-effects-on.jpg "Polluted Waters Stones and Reflections Visualizing Water Pollution Effects on Aquatic Environments and Climate Change Implications for Environmental Sustainability")

## Fundamentals

International cooperation in fisheries is essential because fish populations don’t respect national boundaries. Many fish species migrate across vast distances, traversing multiple Exclusive Economic Zones (EEZs) and even international waters. If each country manages its fisheries independently, without considering the actions of others, **overfishing** becomes almost inevitable. This is a classic example of the “tragedy of the commons,” where individual self-interest leads to the depletion of a shared resource.

Imagine a [scenario Meaning → A scenario is a potential future outcome based on a coherent set of assumptions, a key tool for analysis on the Prism domain.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/)  where one nation heavily fishes a stock that spawns in its waters but migrates into the waters of several other nations. If the first nation decimates the spawning stock, all the other nations will suffer reduced catches, regardless of how sustainably they manage their own fisheries. This interconnectedness demands a coordinated approach.

![A mix of tan and blue short tube-like objects suggests sustainable straws for plastic waste reduction and mitigation of pollution impacts. Varying tones convey the effect of biodegradation processes addressing environmental sustainability and climate change. They are bio based alternatives highlighting circular economy and improved resource management reducing the carbon footprint offering a more sustainable consumption choice.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/analyzing-biodegradable-straws-impacts-on-sustainable-solutions-for-plastic.jpg "Analyzing Biodegradable Straws Impacts on Sustainable Solutions for Plastic Waste Reduction Initiatives Climate Change Mitigation")

### Why Global Agreements Matter

International agreements provide a framework for countries to work together to manage shared fish stocks. These agreements can take many forms, from bilateral treaties between neighboring countries to multilateral conventions involving dozens of nations. The key is to establish common goals, such as maintaining healthy fish populations and ensuring sustainable harvests, and to agree on mechanisms for achieving those goals.

International agreements are vital for managing shared fish stocks by setting common goals and establishing mechanisms for achieving them.

These mechanisms often include:

Without such agreements, the incentive for individual nations to overfish is simply too great. Each nation fears that if it doesn’t catch the fish, someone else will, leading to a race to the bottom that benefits no one in the long run.

![Deconstructed elements arranged with wisps of smoke embody a focus on waste reduction. Sustainable design emphasizes environmental stewardship. The scene evokes a push toward resource efficiency and climate neutrality, signaling a transition to the circular economy and showcasing sustainable building materials.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-deconstruction-an-artistic-study-of-climate-resilience-in-the.jpg "Sustainable Deconstruction an Artistic Study of Climate Resilience in the Circular Economy Transition")

### The Role of Regional Fisheries Management Organizations (RFMOs)

A crucial aspect of [international cooperation Meaning → International Cooperation, within the realm of sustainability, denotes the collaborative synergy among nations, governmental bodies, non-governmental organizations, and diverse stakeholders across borders.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/area/international-cooperation/)  is the establishment and operation of [Regional Fisheries Management Organizations Meaning → International organizations managing fishing in specific regions for sustainable fish stocks.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/term/regional-fisheries-organizations/)  (RFMOs). These organizations bring together countries with an interest in managing fisheries in a particular region or for a particular species. RFMOs play a vital role in setting catch limits, monitoring fishing activity, and enforcing regulations.

For example, the International Commission for the Conservation of Atlantic Tunas (ICCAT) is responsible for managing tuna and tuna-like species in the Atlantic Ocean and its adjacent seas. ICCAT sets quotas for different tuna stocks, monitors catches, and conducts research to assess the health of these populations. Similarly, the North Atlantic Fisheries Organization (NAFO) manages fisheries in the Northwest Atlantic, setting quotas for groundfish species such as cod and haddock.

RFMOs are not without their challenges. They often face difficulties in reaching consensus among member states, and enforcement of regulations can be weak in some cases. However, they represent the best available mechanism for managing fisheries on a regional scale and preventing overfishing.

![This abstract sculpture embodies climate action, showcasing a central blue sphere surrounded by diverse extensions symbolizing environmental sustainability. Textures representing weathered stone and cracked concrete illustrate the urgency of mitigating climate change impacts. This thought-provoking piece seeks to inspire dialogue about carbon footprint reduction, climate resilience strategies, renewable energy transition, ecological restoration and resource conservation.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-sphere-a-conceptual-sculpture-depicting-climate-change-and.jpg "Sustainable Sphere a Conceptual Sculpture Depicting Climate Change and Environmental Responsibility Striving towards Ecological Balance")

### Why Developing Nations Need Support

International cooperation also involves providing support to developing nations to help them manage their fisheries sustainably. Many [developing countries Meaning → Nations with evolving economies, facing unique sustainability challenges due to historical and systemic global inequalities.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/term/developing-countries/)  rely heavily on fisheries for food security and livelihoods, but they often lack the resources and expertise to effectively manage these resources. Developed countries can provide financial assistance, technical support, and training to help developing nations improve their fisheries management practices.

This support can take many forms, such as:

By [investing Meaning → Investing, through an ESG lens, involves allocating capital to companies that demonstrate strong environmental, social, and governance practices.](https://esg.sustainability-directory.com/)  in [sustainable fisheries management Meaning → Sustainable Fisheries Management balances ecological, economic, and social factors to ensure long-term fish availability and healthy marine ecosystems.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/term/sustainable-fisheries-management/)  in developing countries, we can help to ensure that these resources are available for future generations and that the livelihoods of millions of people are protected.

![Abstract art conveys the challenge of environmental balance amidst climate change. Stacked cylinders of varying hues, a resilient plant, and symbolic orbiting lines evoke planetary fragility. The piece captures challenges such as ecological footprint carbon footprint, and environmental stewardship.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/abstract-environmental-balance-art-climate-change-and-sustainable-development-a.jpg "Abstract Environmental Balance Art Climate Change and Sustainable Development a Symbolic Cascade of Sustainability")

## Intermediate

Moving beyond the fundamental need for international cooperation in fisheries due to migratory stocks, a more complex understanding emerges when considering the interconnectedness of [marine ecosystems Meaning → Marine Ecosystems: Interconnected communities of marine life and their environment, vital for planet’s health and human well-being.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/term/marine-ecosystems/)  and the escalating impacts of climate change. Isolated national efforts, even with the best intentions, are often insufficient to address these broader challenges.

![A fractured earth globe symbolizes the environmental crisis and its urgency. The shattered sphere evokes a sense of climate change and ecological disruption stemming from unsustainable resource management. Urgent climate action is necessary.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/fractured-globe-symbolizing-environmental-crisis-sustainable-solutions-and-the.jpg "Fractured Globe Symbolizing Environmental Crisis Sustainable Solutions and the Impact of Climate Change on the Biosphere")

### Ecosystem-Based Management

Traditional [fisheries management Meaning → Fisheries Management, within the sphere of sustainability, denotes the strategic and science-based approach to overseeing and regulating fishing activities.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/area/fisheries-management/)  often focuses on single species, setting catch limits based on the population dynamics of that species alone. However, this approach ignores the complex interactions within marine ecosystems. An ecosystem-based approach, on the other hand, considers the entire ecosystem, including predator-prey relationships, habitat requirements, and the impacts of fishing on non-target species.

Implementing ecosystem-based management requires a high degree of international cooperation. It necessitates sharing data on a wide range of species and habitats, coordinating research efforts, and developing management strategies that take into account the needs of the entire ecosystem. For example, managing a forage fish stock like herring requires considering its role as a food source for larger predators like cod and seabirds. This may involve setting lower catch limits for herring to ensure that there is enough food available for these other species.

The **Benguela Current Large Marine Ecosystem (BCLME)** program is a good example of an international effort to implement ecosystem-based management. This program brings together Angola, Namibia, and South Africa to manage the resources of the Benguela Current, one of the most productive marine ecosystems in the world. The BCLME program focuses on understanding the interactions between different components of the ecosystem and developing management strategies that promote the sustainable use of all its resources.

![The damaged planet form symbolizes climate change impacts and the urgent need for climate action and sustainable practices to reduce our carbon footprint. Green shoots represent environmental resilience and hope for ecological conservation in the face of global warming and climate change. Emanating wisps represent pollution and call attention to planetary health as essential to long-term biodiversity and our overall wellbeing.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/symbolic-representation-of-climate-change-impact-on-planetary-health-and.jpg "Symbolic Representation of Climate Change Impact on Planetary Health and Environmental Sustainability")

### Addressing Illegal, Unreported, and Unregulated (IUU) Fishing

Illegal, unreported, and unregulated (IUU) fishing poses a significant threat to the [sustainability Meaning → Sustainability is the capacity for human civilization to coexist with the Earth's biosphere in a state of equilibrium.](https://sustainability-directory.com/)  of fisheries worldwide. [IUU fishing Meaning → IUU Fishing denotes Illegal, Unreported, and Unregulated fishing activities.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/area/iuu-fishing/)  undermines efforts to manage fisheries sustainably, depletes fish stocks, and harms marine ecosystems. It also has significant economic impacts, costing billions of dollars each year.

Combating IUU fishing requires a concerted international effort. This includes:

The **Port State Measures Agreement (PSMA)** is an international treaty that aims to combat IUU fishing by strengthening port state controls. The PSMA requires countries to inspect foreign vessels entering their ports and to take action against vessels found to be involved in IUU fishing. This agreement has been ratified by many countries and is considered a major step forward in the fight against IUU fishing.

Combating illegal fishing requires a global effort involving strengthened monitoring systems, improved port controls, and international cooperation.

![The sphere within a porous stone structure reflects the Earth's fragility and the impact of climate change. A grainy texture and cratered surface symbolize environmental degradation. The light green hue indicates potential for ecological restoration.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-abstract-art-demonstrating-ecological-interdependence-and-fragility.jpg "Sustainable Abstract Art Demonstrating Ecological Interdependence and Fragility in a World Facing Climate Change and Environmental Degradation")

### The Impacts of Climate Change on Fisheries

Climate change is having profound impacts on marine ecosystems and fisheries worldwide. Rising ocean temperatures, ocean acidification, and changes in ocean currents are altering the distribution and abundance of fish stocks. Some species are shifting their ranges poleward in response to warming waters, while others are experiencing declines in productivity due to changes in food availability.

Addressing the impacts of [climate change Meaning → Climate Change: Long-term shifts in global climate patterns, primarily due to human activities, causing widespread environmental and societal impacts.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/term/climate-change/)  on fisheries requires a global effort to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and to adapt to the changes that are already occurring. This includes:

International cooperation is essential for addressing the global challenge of climate change. This includes sharing information on the impacts of climate change on fisheries, coordinating research efforts, and developing joint strategies for adapting to these changes.

![This intricate cellular structure symbolizes bioengineered approaches for environmental sustainability, demonstrating the role of climate technology to promote ecological restoration and achieve a low carbon future. The image signifies potential progress towards carbon sequestration and reducing the impacts of climate change, visualizing carbon footprint reduction through microbial ecological techniques. Emphasizing climate action the goal is to enhance planetary health and demonstrate effective environmental stewardship using innovations in sustainable climate science.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/illustration-depicting-carbon-sequestration-and-bioengineered-solutions-for.jpg "Illustration Depicting Carbon Sequestration and Bioengineered Solutions for Addressing Climate Change Sustainability")

### The Role of International Law

International law provides a framework for cooperation in fisheries management. The **United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS)** is the primary international legal instrument governing the use of the oceans. UNCLOS establishes the rights and responsibilities of states with respect to the conservation and management of marine resources.

UNCLOS requires states to cooperate in the management of shared fish stocks and to take measures to prevent overfishing. It also establishes the concept of the Exclusive Economic Zone (EEZ), which gives coastal states sovereign rights over the resources within 200 nautical miles of their coastlines. However, UNCLOS also recognizes the rights of other states to fish in the EEZs of coastal states, subject to certain conditions.

In addition to UNCLOS, there are a number of other international agreements that address specific aspects of fisheries management. These include the Convention on Biological Diversity, the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species (CITES), and the Agreement for the Implementation of the Provisions of UNCLOS Relating to the Conservation and Management of Straddling Fish Stocks and Highly Migratory Fish Stocks (the Fish Stocks Agreement).

![A polished sphere nestled in a bowl represents sustainable practices and ecological balance needed to secure future earth resources. This image symbolizes responsible consumption, circular economy, climate action and resource efficiency emphasizing our commitment to environmental stewardship to minimize carbon footprint. Light and color are used to enhance a global vision of net zero emissions, ecological integrity, biodiversity conservation, promoting collaborative efforts toward environmental protection and planetary health ultimately aiding climate resilience.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/sustainable-sphere-of-future-earth-resources-embracing-ecological-balance-and.jpg "Sustainable Sphere of Future Earth Resources Embracing Ecological Balance and Mitigating Climate Change Risks")

## Academic

The necessity of international cooperation in fisheries transcends mere resource management; it is fundamentally intertwined with global sustainability imperatives. Scholarly discourse increasingly frames fisheries [governance Meaning → Governance, in an ESG context, refers to the systems of rules, practices, and processes by which a company is directed and controlled.](https://esg.sustainability-directory.com/)  as a complex adaptive system, demanding integrated, transdisciplinary approaches that acknowledge ecological interconnectedness, socioeconomic dependencies, and the ethical dimensions of resource allocation. A critical analysis reveals that the effectiveness of international fisheries cooperation hinges on addressing power asymmetries, incorporating local ecological knowledge, and fostering adaptive governance frameworks capable of responding to emergent challenges like climate-induced species redistribution.

![A landscape illustrates water contamination using a light blue stream flowing between piles of black rock indicating soil degradation and polluted runoff with harmful substances. This visually signifies water pollution's effect due to climate change threatening environmental sustainability and biodiversity, reflecting the urgent need for climate action to promote environmental remediation, habitat restoration and sustainable resource management focusing on water resources. Industrial waste is an immediate challenge, and sustainable water treatment is essential for long-term health, protecting against potential ecosystem services collapse.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/environmental-impact-a-close-up-look-at-blue-contaminated-water-pollution.jpg "Environmental Impact a Close up Look at Blue Contaminated Water Pollution Highlighting Climate Change Risks and Ecological Sustainability")

### What Are the Limitations of Current RFMOs?

While Regional [Fisheries Management Organizations Meaning → International organizations managing fishing in specific regions for sustainable fish stocks.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/term/regional-fisheries-organizations/)  (RFMOs) represent a cornerstone of international fisheries governance, their efficacy is often hampered by several systemic limitations. Research highlights persistent challenges related to → (1) **enforcement deficits**, stemming from inadequate monitoring and surveillance capabilities, coupled with weak sanctioning mechanisms; (2) **scientific uncertainty**, particularly concerning stock assessments and ecosystem dynamics, leading to contested catch limits; (3) **political influence**, where short-term economic interests of member states overshadow long-term sustainability goals; and (4) **lack of inclusivity**, with limited participation from small-scale fishers and coastal communities whose livelihoods are directly affected by RFMO decisions. Addressing these limitations requires a paradigm shift towards more transparent, participatory, and ecologically informed governance structures.

A 2020 study published in Marine Policy examined the performance of five major tuna RFMOs, finding significant discrepancies between scientific advice and management decisions, often driven by political and economic considerations. The study concluded that strengthening the independence and accountability of RFMOs is crucial for achieving sustainable tuna fisheries.

The following table shows the limitations:

| Limitation | Description | Impact |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Enforcement Deficits | Inadequate monitoring and weak sanctions | Increased IUU fishing and non-compliance |
| Scientific Uncertainty | Uncertainty in stock assessments | Contested catch limits and overfishing |
| Political Influence | Economic interests over sustainability | Compromised conservation efforts |
| Lack of Inclusivity | Limited participation from local communities | Marginalization of affected stakeholders |

![A frosty tendril embodies resilience in the face of climate change impact on the environment. Emphasizing sustainable solutions such as those involved in the circular economy this close-up invites reflection on climate adaptation, resilience, environmental protection and the interconnectedness of natural resources to inform sustainable land management policies. The scene invokes concepts of climate vulnerability and ecosystem services protection needed to ensure the long-term health of our planet given growing air pollution and related threats that can be addressed with proper climate change mitigation strategies and careful carbon footprint analysis.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/frozen-tendril-resilience-climate-adaptation-strategy-showing-circular-economy.jpg "Frozen Tendril Resilience Climate Adaptation Strategy Showing Circular Economy Potential in Sustainable Land Management")

### How Can International Law Adapt to Climate Change Impacts?

The accelerating impacts of climate change necessitate a fundamental re-evaluation of international fisheries law. The current legal framework, largely based on static geographical boundaries and historical fishing patterns, is ill-equipped to address the dynamic shifts in species distribution and ecosystem functioning driven by climate change. Key areas for adaptation include → (1) **revising allocation principles** to account for climate-induced stock movements, potentially requiring the establishment of “climate-adaptive” quotas; (2) **strengthening cooperation** on transboundary fisheries management, particularly for stocks migrating across multiple jurisdictions due to climate change; (3) **integrating climate change considerations** into stock assessments and management plans, using predictive modeling to anticipate [future Meaning → Exploring future scenarios and possibilities is the core focus of the Prism sustainability satellite.](https://prism.sustainability-directory.com/)  impacts; and (4) **developing legal mechanisms** for addressing climate-related displacement of fishing communities and ensuring equitable access to resources. The Fish Stocks Agreement (UNFSA) and UNCLOS provide a foundation, but require innovative interpretation and implementation to effectively address climate change challenges.

International law must adapt to climate change by revising allocation principles, strengthening cooperation, and integrating climate considerations into management plans.

For instance, a 2022 report by the International Council on Environmental Law (ICEL) proposes the development of a “climate change protocol” to UNFSA, outlining specific obligations for states to cooperate on climate-adaptive fisheries management. This protocol would address issues such as data sharing, joint research, and the establishment of transboundary management plans for climate-vulnerable stocks.

![An abstract composition uses citrus slices surrounded by a dark ring and smoke to represent the impact of climate change and unsustainable practices. The fruit is bisected with smog within its core highlighting issues regarding resource depletion and carbon emissions. The fragments adorning the ring symbolize ecosystem fragmentation and a plea for sustainable practices, conservation biology, and ecosystem integrity.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/abstract-circular-composition-with-citrus-slice-dark-smoke-and-black-ring.jpg "Abstract Circular Composition with Citrus Slice Dark Smoke and Black Ring Symbolizing Climate Change")

### What Role Does Local Ecological Knowledge Play?

The integration of [local ecological knowledge LEK’s limits lie in its scope, subjectivity, and adaptability, yet it offers invaluable, context-specific insights for sustainability when properly integrated.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/question/what-are-the-limits-of-local-ecological-knowledge/)  (LEK) is increasingly recognized as a critical element of effective fisheries governance. LEK, accumulated over generations by fishing communities, provides valuable insights into fish behavior, habitat use, and ecosystem dynamics that are often overlooked by conventional scientific assessments. Incorporating LEK into fisheries management can enhance the accuracy of stock assessments, improve the effectiveness of management measures, and foster greater stakeholder participation and compliance.

However, challenges remain in bridging the epistemological divide between LEK and scientific knowledge, ensuring the equitable representation of diverse knowledge systems, and developing participatory research methodologies that respect the cultural context of LEK. International cooperation can play a crucial role in promoting the recognition and integration of LEK in fisheries management, through the development of best practices, the sharing of case studies, and the provision of technical and financial support to community-based monitoring programs.

The following list shows the benefits of LEK:

A 2018 study in [Ecology Meaning → Ecology is the scientific study of interactions among organisms and their environment, a core principle of the Eco domain.](https://eco.sustainability-directory.com/)  and Society examined the use of LEK in fisheries management in the Pacific Islands, finding that incorporating traditional knowledge significantly improved the accuracy of stock assessments and led to more effective management outcomes. The study emphasized the importance of building trust and fostering collaborative relationships between scientists and local communities.

![A stylized globe representing Earth is contained by a wire frame. Snowfall hints at climate change disrupting environmental stability. The structure emphasizes global sustainability and the vulnerability of ecosystems.](https://climate.sustainability-directory.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/global-environmental-change-conceptual-globe-enclosed-in-wire-frame-visualizing.jpg "Global Environmental Change Conceptual Globe Enclosed in Wire Frame Visualizing Planetary Sustainability Amidst Winter Snowfall")

### How Can Power Asymmetries Be Addressed?

Power asymmetries among states and stakeholders represent a significant impediment to effective international fisheries cooperation. Developed nations, with their advanced scientific capabilities and economic resources, often exert disproportionate influence in RFMOs and other international forums, shaping management decisions in ways that may disadvantage developing countries and small-scale fishing communities. Addressing these [power Meaning → Power is the rate at which energy is generated and consumed, with a focus on transitioning to clean and renewable sources.](https://energy.sustainability-directory.com/)  imbalances requires a multi-pronged approach, including → (1) **strengthening the capacity** of developing countries to participate effectively in international negotiations and scientific assessments; (2) **promoting greater transparency** and accountability in decision-making processes; (3) **ensuring equitable access** to financial and technical resources for [sustainable fisheries Meaning → Sustainable Fisheries refers to the practice of harvesting fish and seafood in a manner that does not compromise the long-term health and abundance of th

SUMMARY:
We build strategic partnerships with other nations, particularly with developing countries, to promote sustainable and responsible management of fisheries.

FULL CONTENT:

# International Affairs

## International Affairs

U.S. fisheries are a global model of success. We are leading the way in science-based management and in the use of innovative strategies to achieve and maintain sustainable fisheries and to preserve and rebuild protected species, including marine mammals and sea turtles.

As one of the largest seafood-consuming and fishing nations, it is critical that we take an active role in shaping the conservation and management regimes of international fisheries. We work to meet the demand of U.S. consumers that imported seafood be safe, legal, and sustainable and ensure confidence in U.S. seafood by protecting and strengthening the seafood market through international engagement.

We do this by engaging with other nations bilaterally and multilaterally through various international fisheries organizations and agreements to promote sound management and conservation of global fisheries resources in a manner consistent with U.S. domestic fisheries policy.

## More Information

### International Species Conservation and Management

Species are distributed throughout the world’s oceans, so their conservation and recovery requires collaborative action on a global scale. Learn more about ongoing measures taken toward the international conservation and management of [sharks](/national/international-affairs/shark-conservation), [marine mammals](/national/international-affairs/international-marine-mammal-conservation), [tunas](/foreign/international-affairs/international-tuna-conservation), [sea turtles](/national/international-affairs/international-sea-turtle-conservation), [nautilus](/species/chambered-nautilus), [seabirds](/national/international-affairs/reducing-seabird-bycatch-international-waters), and the issue of [international bycatch](/topic/bycatch/conservation-and-management).

### Seafood Commerce and Trade

We work to ensure confidence in U.S. seafood by protecting and strengthening the seafood market through global trade, establishing partnerships with industry and consumer groups, and providing voluntary seafood inspection services.

[Learn more about seafood commerce and trade](/topic/seafood-commerce-and-trade)

### Marine Mammal Import Provisions Rule

The Marine Mammal Protection Act Import Import Provisions rule implements aspects of the MMPA that aim to reduce marine mammal bycatch associated with international commercial fishing operations by requiring nations exporting fish and fish products to the United States be held to the same standards as U.S. commercial fishing operations.

[Learn more about the MMPA Import Provisions rule](/foreign/bycatch/international-protected-species-and-bycatch-mitigation)

### Seafood Import Monitoring Program

For imports of certain seafood products, there are reporting and recordkeeping requirements needed to prevent [illegally caught, unreported, unregulated](/topic/international-affairs/iuu-fishing), and/or misrepresented seafood from entering U.S. commerce. This provides additional protections for our national economy, global food security, and the sustainability of our shared ocean resources.

[Learn more about the Seafood Import Monitoring Program](/international/seafood-import-monitoring-program)

### Permits

We administer various programs requiring federal permits to authorize commercial and recreational fishing within and beyond U.S. waters. We coordinate issuance of high seas fishing permits, foreign fishing permits, Antarctic marine living resource trade permits, and international permits. Additionally, we participate in the [National Permit System](https://fisheriespermits.noaa.gov/npspub/pub_cmn_login/index_live.jsp), a government service portal for electronically available permits.”

[Learn more about permits](/fishing-and-seafood-permits?fishing_permits[1000008636]=1000008636)

### Collaborative Accelerator for Lawful Maritime Conditions in Seafood

CALM-CS (pronounced “calm seas”)was developed to promote legal and safe working conditions throughout the fishing and seafood industry.

[Learn more about the initiative](/international-affairs/collaborative-accelerator-lawful-maritime-conditions-seafood)

## International Collaboration

Fish and other marine animals travel beyond national boundaries.

![750x500-fish-school-underwater.jpg](/s3//styles/original/s3/dam-migration/750x500-fish-school-underwater.jpg?itok=KKneemyf)

## Engagement with International Community

Because fish and other marine wildlife cross national boundaries, the United States shares living marine resources with other countries. The way other countries manage these shared marine resources can directly affect the status of fish stocks and protected or endangered species of importance to the United States. For this reason, we collaborate with other countries to ensure successful fisheries management and conservation practices.

### Regional Agreements

We participate in various [international fisheries organizations](https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/national/international-affairs/regional-international-agreements) that promote international cooperation to achieve effective, responsible marine stewardship and ensure sustainable fisheries management. Learn more about the international fisheries organizations that NOAA Fisheries participates in for each region:

[Atlantic Ocean](/national/international-affairs/regional-international-agreements#atlantic-ocean-regional-fisheries-management-organizations)

[Indian Ocean](/national/international-affairs/regional-international-agreements#indian-ocean-intergovernmental-organizations)

[Pacific Ocean](/national/international-affairs/regional-international-agreements#pacific-ocean-regional-fisheries-management-organizationshttp://www.nmfs.noaa.gov/ia/agreements/regional_agreements/pacific/13_pacific_rfmo.html/national/international-affairs/regional-international-agreements#pacific-ocean-regional-fisheries-management-organizations)

[Southern Ocean](/national/international-affairs/regional-international-agreements#southern-ocean-intergovernmental-organizations)

#### In the Spotlight: International Commission for the Conservation of Atlantic Tunas

The [International Commission for the Conservation of Atlantic Tunas](/national/international-affairs/international-commission-conservation-atlantic-tunas), also known as ICCAT, was created in 1969 and covers 50 member nations, including the EU. ICCAT covers the Atlantic Ocean and deals with tuna and tuna-like species.

The Advisory Committee to the U.S. Section of ICCAT is made up of people representing commercial industries, recreational fishing groups, and academia, who are nominated every two years and provide advice to the U.S. ICCAT Commissioners on international issues related to conservation and management.

### Global Agreements

We have the authority to engage in international activities with other countries to implement the [Magnuson-Stevens Act](https://beta.fisheries.noaa.gov/topic/laws-policies). Our international affairs work builds strategic partnerships with other nations, particularly with developing countries, to promote sustainable and responsible management of fisheries and other relevant marine resources at a global level.

[Learn more about our current global and other multilateral living marine resource agreements](/national/international-affairs/global-and-other-multilateral-living-marine-resource-agreements)

### Bilateral Arrangements

We hold consultations and strategic bilateral meetings with a number of countries and fishing entities on a regular basis to exchange views and enlist support for U.S. fisheries trade and conservation and management initiatives in international fora.

[More about NOAA’s bilateral and other international agreements](https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/resource/document/2023-fisheries-international-agreement-book)

### Fisheries Trade Agreements and Organizations

As a large seafood-consuming and fishing nation, it is critical that we take an active role in shaping the conservation and management regimes of international fisheries. We work to meet the demand of U.S. consumers that imported seafood be safe, legal, and sustainable and ensure confidence in U.S. seafood by protecting and strengthening the seafood market through global trade.

[Learn more about our international fisheries trade agreements](/international/international-affairs/international-trade-organizations)

## More Information

## Regulatory Programs

NOAA has regulatory and stewardship authority for fisheries, marine sanctuaries, marine mammals, threatened and endangered species, and habitat conservation. We are engaged in a range of regulatory activities to protect and restore these important resources on a global scale. We also aim to level the playing field for United States fishermen who operate in some of the most sustainably managed and heavily regulated fisheries in the world.

### Trade Monitoring

As part of our mission to sustainably manage fishery resources, we implement international trade monitoring programs initiated by international fishery management organizations or required by domestic law. Effective September 20, 2016, we’ve established regulations to integrate the collection of trade documentation within the International Trade Data System, requiring electronic information through a single automated portal.

[Learn more about our foreign fishery trade data](/national/sustainable-fisheries/foreign-fishery-trade-data)

We work with U.S. Customs and Border Protection to provide support to seafood dealers to ensure imported seafood, such as Patagonian toothfish, bluefin tuna, swordfish, and bigeye tuna, is caught and imported legally. Our trade monitoring programs include the [Seafood Import Monitoring Program](https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/international/seafood-import-monitoring-program), the [Antarctic Marine Living Resources Program](https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/permit/antarctic-marine-living-resource-permits), the [Tuna Tracking and Verification Program](https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/national/marine-mammal-protection/dolphin-safe), and [the Atlantic Highly Migratory Species International Trade Program](https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/national/atlantic-highly-migratory-species/atlantic-highly-migratory-species-international-trade).

### Marine Mammal Import Act Import Provisions

The Marine Mammal Protection Act Import Provisions rule implements aspects of the MMPA that aim to reduce marine mammal bycatch associated with international commercial fishing operations, by requiring nations exporting fish and fish products to the United States to be held to the same standards as United States commercial fishing operations.

[Learn about the MMPA Import Provisions Rule](/feature-story/noaa-fisheries-establishes-international-marine-mammal-bycatch-criteria-us-imports)

### Identification of IUU Fishing, Bycatch, and Shark Catch Activities

Under the Magnuson-Stevens Reauthorization Act, the United States identifies nations and entities whose fishing vessels were reported to be engaged in [illegal, unreported, and unregulated fishing, bycatch, and shark catch activities](/foreign/international-affairs/identification-iuu-fishing-activities) and consults with those countries to improve their fisheries management and enforcement practices.

[Learn more about nations and entities identified and any corrective actions taken](/national/international-affairs/identification-iuu-fishing-activities)

### Seafood Import Monitoring Program

For imports of certain seafood products, we establish reporting and recordkeeping requirements needed to prevent [illegally caught, unreported, unregulated](/topic/international-affairs/iuu-fishing), and/or misrepresented seafood from entering U.S. commerce, providing additional protections for our national economy, global food security and the sustainability of our shared ocean resources.

[Learn more about the Seafood Import Monitoring Program and its requirements](/international/seafood-import-monitoring-program)

Additionally, we work with U.S. Customs and Border Protection to provide support to seafood dealers to ensure imported seafood, such as Patagonian toothfish, bluefin tuna, swordfish, and bigeye tuna, is caught and imported legally.

[Learn more about Dolphin Safe Tuna (Tuna Tracking and Verification Program)](/national/marine-mammal-protection/dolphin-safe)

## More Information

## International Cooperation on Key Issues

We build strategic partnerships with other nations, particularly with developing countries, to promote sustainable and responsible management of fisheries and other relevant marine resources at the national, regional, and global levels.

### Monitoring, Control, and Surveillance

We assist in the compliance to fishery management measures, ensuring that appropriate controls are set, monitored, and complied with. We encourage various international efforts and programs to more effectively enforce conservation measures designed to protect world fisheries and ecosystems.

Learn more about [international monitoring, control, and surveillance efforts](/foreign/international-affairs/international-monitoring-control-and-surveillance)

### Protected Species and Bycatch Mitigation

Bycatch, a major threat to sustainable populations, poses a particular challenge in developing countries with fisheries that export to the United States. Data to document bycatch and the effects of bycatch are often lacking in these countries as the research needed takes time, money, and training that are often limited. We support various bilateral and multilateral cooperative efforts that work on the ground to reduce bycatch, including data collection efforts and projects to improve bycatch mitigation technologies.

[Learn more about international protected species and bycatch mitigation efforts](/foreign/bycatch/international-protected-species-and-bycatch-mitigation)

### Improving Fisheries Governance

The lack of information and oversight in fisheries management among some developing nations  has resulted in declining marine stocks, many of which are vital to providing fish to the local markets. We encourage various fisheries governance projects and programs to achieve effective and responsible marine stewardship and ensure sustainable global fisheries management. These opportunities build international cooperation that supports science-based fisheries management and strengthens our international strategic partnerships.

[Learn more about international fisheries governance efforts](/foreign/international-affairs/international-improving-fisheries-governance)

### International Species Conservation and Management

Species are distributed throughout the world’s oceans, so their conservation and recovery requires collaborative action on a global scale. Learn more about ongoing measures nations are taking toward the international conservation and management of [sharks](https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/national/international-affairs/shark-conservation), [marine mammals](https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/national/international-affairs/international-marine-mammal-conservation), [tunas](https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/foreign/international-affairs/international-tuna-conservation), [sea turtles](https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/national/international-affairs/international-sea-turtle-conservation), [nautilus](https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/species/chambered-nautilus), [seabirds](https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/national/international-affairs/reducing-seabird-bycatch-international-waters), and the issue of [international bycatch](https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/foreign/bycatch/international-protected-species-and-bycatch-mitigation).

## More Information

## IUU Fishing

Illegal, unreported, and unregulated fishing describes a wide variety of fishing-related activities that may violate both national and international fishing regulations. They can occur at several points in the seafood supply chain. Examples of IUU fishing include:

These activities significantly undermine U.S. and global efforts to sustainably manage fisheries and conserve marine resources. As a result, IUU fishing threatens food security and upsets the economies of coastal communities around the world. It’s difficult to quantify the full global economic impacts of these activities. However, there is little disagreement that the loss is in the billions, or even tens of billions, of dollars each year.

IUU fishing also puts rule-abiding fishermen and seafood producers in the United States at a disadvantage in global markets. Fishermen who engage in IUU fishing activities avoid the operational costs associated with sustainable fishery management and may harvest more fish and seafood than is allowed. As a result, they can sell their products for less money.

[Learn more about IUU fishing](/insight/understanding-illegal-unreported-and-unregulated-fishing)

### Our Work and Interagency Collaboration

Combating IUU fishing is a top priority for the United States. NOAA Fisheries is one of several federal agencies working together to effectively address this critical issue. Coordination among U.S. government agencies is critical to effectively combating IUU fishing. So are our collaborations with foreign governments and entities, international organizations, non-government organizations, and the private sector.

[Learn about the U.S. Interagency Working Group on IUU Fishing](/national/us-interagency-working-group-iuu-fishing)

#### Keeping IUU Fish and Fish Products Out of U.S. Markets

The United States imports nearly 80 percent of its seafood. That means it’s essential to ensure that our high demand for imported seafood does not create incentives for IUU fishing activity. We do this by taking steps, in collaboration with other nations, that prevent IUU fish and fish products from entering our markets:

[Efforts and agreements to prevent IUU fish and fish products from entering the United States](/international/international-affairs/combating-iuu-fishing-world-fisheries)

#### Strengthening Global Marine Stewardship

Successfully combating a global problem like IUU fishing requires multilateral cooperation. This includes strong regional fisheries management organizations and strict monitoring, control, and surveillance of the ocean areas they manage. Our partnerships and agreements with foreign governments, foreign entities, and international organizations are crucial to making counter-IUU fishing efforts successful. They allow us to promote the implementation of sustainable fisheries regulations and effective monitoring systems overseas to combat these activities.

[International and regional fisheries management organizations](/international-affairs/international-and-regional-fisheries-management-organizations)

[List of international fisheries agreements](/resource/document/2020-international-fisheries-agreement-book)

[International engagement and cooperation](/topic/international-affairs)

#### Helping Nations Build Monitoring and Enforcement Capacity

We work closely with other nations, particularly with developing countries, to help them enhance their abilities to combat IUU fishing. We provide the tools, resources, information, and information sharing avenues they need to be successful. We do this primarily by providing technical assistance and hosting training workshops for foreign agencies, organizations, and communities.

[Technical assistance and capacity building](https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/enforcement-efforts-combat-illegal-unreported-and-unregulated-fishing#counter-iuu-fishing-technical-assistance-and-capacity-building)

### Success Stories

## More Information

## International Fisheries Science

Our nation’s marine life has no geopolitical boundaries. Yet, our actions—and those of other nations—can have a significant impact on the conservation and sustainability of those resources. Managing our nation’s resources requires a strong scientific and management enterprise strengthened by our ability to work effectively with our international partners. NOAA Fisheries’ international science activities are a critical component for providing the best available science to inform and guide marine life conservation.

### International Collaboration Supports Domestic Fisheries

![NOAA Ship BELL SHIMADA during the 2010 Pacific Hake Inter-Vessel Calibration. Credit: Stephen de Blois]()

NOAA Fisheries’ science enterprise is diverse. It currently relies on significant international collaborations to keep abreast of cutting-edge innovations and to exchange experience and knowledge with internationally recognized experts. Our scientists are recognized experts in a wide range of disciplines. They participate in various international scientific forums, Regional Fishery Management Organizations, and [bilateral collaborations with foreign partners](/international/international-affairs/bilateral-agreements).

We want to ensure that the United States benefits from cutting-edge science from around the world. We also want NOAA’s expertise and innovative scientific approaches to fisheries management to be shared in international forums.

[International science storymap](https://noaa.maps.arcgis.com/apps/Cascade/index.html?appid=4d564180d72949d2bb246615e97e509d)

![screenshot of international science storymap ]()

NOAA Fisheries seeks to:

### Regional Fishery Management Organizations

NOAA Fisheries scientists participate in many scientific committees that advise regional fishery management organizations in setting fisheries management and marine conservation actions. Participation in these organizations support cutting-edge science, provide science advice, and serve both U.S. domestic and international interests. They also provide an important venue for developing and fostering research collaborations and scientific exchanges.

[Organizations NOAA Fisheries participates in for each region](https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/international-affairs/international-fisheries-organizations)

### Marine Science Organizations

NOAA Fisheries is heavily involved in a variety of international marine science organizations. These groups provide a venue for fishery scientists to collaborate, share research methodologies and approaches, and address new challenges impacting fisheries and marine life.

[Marine science organizations NOAA Fisheries collaborates with and the work they do](https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/international/international-affairs/marine-science-organizations)
